body: Related issue on microsoft/vscode#193073

I was searching for this issue for evenings now - bruh.
As mentioned here, this issue occurs on Windows but not on linux.

It might also be worth mentioning that this issue only occurs for characters which have a code point above `U+FFFF` (i.e. any nerd font glyph which has an ID with more than 4 hexadecimal characters).

Meaning:

The folder icon `nf-md-folder` with the code point `f024b` will cause this issue, while the (I believe) similar looking icon `nf-fa-folder` with code point `f07b` (take note that it only has 4 characters) won't:
![image](https://github.com/starship/starship/assets/7085564/65e5647d-d7bc-40d2-8630-60d62ad0324d)

I understand. May I know why this only happens to Visual Studio Code Terminal not Windows Terminal?

I assume it's an error in Visual Studio's integrated terminal.

I believe we should move over the comments from here to vscode.

> I understand. May I know why this only happens to Visual Studio Code Terminal not Windows Terminal?


Windows terminal has also appeared. It seems that it will appear more frequently when multiple shells are opened, but no reproducible path has been found yet.

Fricking heck, okay it's getting even more confusing:
It works in WSL properly.
So I come to believe that it has to be a PowerShell Core issue (?)

> > I understand. May I know why this only happens to Visual Studio Code Terminal not Windows Terminal?
> 
> Windows terminal has also appeared. It seems that it will appear more frequently when multiple shells are opened, but no reproducible path has been found yet.

As far as I can tell, it can be reproduced 100% by trying to print any UTF8 character with a code point > 0xFFFF.
It also only becomes clearly visible when printing such characters with a background (as it's the case in both our screenshots).

> So I come to believe that it has to be a PowerShell Core issue (?)

I also encountered this problem when using fish. It seems that I use many terminals to output a lot of text at the same time, and then the newly created terminal will have a messed up style. However, I still cannot reproduce it 100%

Ouch - I remember also once having this problem and having it vanish when re-opening the terminal. Although, I do not remember whether it also involved `fish` - I do remember running Windows back then.

But just to make sure that you can reproduce the issue in the same way that we do. Could you please install the theme posted by @fluentmoheshwar and:
  - Try opening a `pwsh` terminal in vscode installed on Windows
  - Try opening vscode in WSL and run `pwsh.exe` in a WSL terminal inside vscode
  - Open a WSL (or WSL Ubuntu) tab in Windows Terminal and run `pwsh.exe` inside it

Because all of them let me 100% reproduce this error
I don't think it's shell specific. I have seen same issue with zsh on windows

> Ouch - I remember also once having this problem and having it vanish when re-opening the terminal. Although, I do not remember whether it also involved `fish` - I do remember running Windows back then.
> 
> But just to make sure that you can reproduce the issue in the same way that we do. Could you please install the theme posted by @fluentmoheshwar and:
>   - Try opening a `pwsh` terminal in vscode installed on Windows
>   - Try opening vscode in WSL and run `pwsh.exe` in a WSL terminal inside vscode
>   - Open a WSL (or WSL Ubuntu) tab in Windows Terminal and run `pwsh.exe` inside it
> 
> Because all of them let me 100% reproduce this error

Also try installing PowerShell Linux version in WSL and connecting to it via WSL extension in VS Code

@fluentmoheshwar switching the windows character per this comment worked for me: https://github.com/starship/starship/issues/5334#issuecomment-2131315871

(note: the character doesn't render in well in the webpage, but will in VSCode and its terminal with a nerd font installed)

if you look carefully, I don't have any windows character.

Occasionally, the terminal on vscode does not display text. After closing all terminals, open a new terminal and it will be fine.

![starship](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/f21760db-2529-4be9-8873-73a05ededcc5)

Changing the OS character fixed it for me.

Using the `nf-md-arch` character (`f08c7`):
![image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/ddfc17ec-d11c-4a62-b080-e58d8ddb79b0)

Using the `nf-linux-archlinux` character (`f303`):
![image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/458a5e95-d73c-4be3-b698-cf76bcff51ea)

Pick yours from the [Nerd Fonts Cheat Sheet](https://www.nerdfonts.com/cheat-sheet).
Keep in mind, though that characters **above `0xffff`** are still not rendered properly as mentioned.

@fluentmoheshwar any Nerd Font character above `0xffff` causes this issue.
The user profile icon you use, for example, namely `nf-md-account_circle` has the code point value `0xf0009`, which is above `0x0ffff`.

If you instead use `nf-fa-circle_user`, which has the code point value `0xf2bd`, you won't see a space next to the `$username` section anymore.

Consider this a workaround until the issue at hand is fixed.

I still have to find out, whether this is only a VSCode issue or entirely a Windows error (hopefully not, bc I wouldn't know where to open up an issue, then, lol)

Edit:
Similarly, you use `nf-md-folder` as your folder icon which has code point value `0xf024b` and the clock icon `nf-md-clock` with code point value `0xf0954` (both > `0x0ffff`).

As a workaround, you would have to swap these icons for other icons with lower code point values, too.

You can btw look up the icon names and their code point values on the nerdfont cheatsheet, too:
[Nerd Fonts Cheat Sheet](https://www.nerdfonts.com/cheat-sheet)

Alright - I tested it on Windows Terminal now, too and there is something interesting I found out:
Just to keep it in mind:
Nerd Font Icons which actually consist of 2 letters (this being icons with code points with a value > `0xffff`) cause spaces which are especially irritating in PowerLines.

In terms of Windows Terminal...
  - `1.12.10983.0`, this error **does** occur
  - `1.20.11781.0`, this error **does not** occur

Furthermore, it occurs in `vscode` editors, but only on Windows.
So this has to be an error in the Terminal Emulators rather than prompt engines such as Starship.
I think it might make sense to open up an issue in the `vscode` repository

So, it looks like Conpty of a number of Windows versions is broken.
There is a workaround for Windows on `vscode` since version `1.93` in August 2024: https://code.visualstudio.com/updates/v1_93#_conpty-shipping-in-product

So basically all you need to do is set `"terminal.integrated.experimental.windowsUseConptyDll": true`

With this clearly being contpy and Windows issue, I'd recommend you to close this issue.
